refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control architecture with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and communication bottlenecks.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDI) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Implement configuration management and rapid reconfiguration capability to adapt system topology in response to detected failures or degraded components.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 (integrated approach): Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover (DP1), supported by predictive maintenance via condition monitoring (DP3) and comprehensive fault detection with automated diagnostics (DP6). This combination addresses both reactive recovery (failover) and proactive prevention (predictive maintenance), minimizing both failure frequency and repair time.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP3 coupling: Redundancy strategy must align with maintenance scheduling—predictive maintenance may recommend component replacement during operation, requiring hot-swap capability (DP2 dependency).
DP1 and DP6 coupling: Failover logic depends on FDI accuracy; false positives trigger unnecessary switchovers, degrading availability; false negatives allow failed units to remain active.
DP3 and DP6 coupling: Condition monitoring data feeds FDI; inconsistent sensor calibration or model drift can cause misalignment between predicted and detected failures.

Implicit dependency on IM boundaries: Redundancy effectiveness depends on physical/logical isolation of redundant subsystems; poor module boundaries (e.g., shared power rails, common control bus) undermine independence.
coupling_check.notes: The three DPs are logically distinct (redundancy, prediction, detection) but operationally interdependent. Coupling is manageable if clear interfaces and ownership boundaries are established at the IM layer. Risk: over-reliance on any single DP without the others will not achieve 0.97 availability target.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be physically or logically isolated without prohibitive cost, weight, or complexity penalties.
ASSUMPTION: Condition monitoring sensors and predictive models are sufficiently accurate (low false-positive/negative rates) to guide maintenance decisions.
ASSUMPTION: Automatic failover logic is deterministic and testable; no race conditions or Byzantine failure modes in switchover decision-making.
ASSUMPTION: MTTR for manual intervention (if required) is compatible with the 0.97 availability target; if MTTR > ~8.76 hours per year, failover alone is insufficient.
RISK: Redundancy increases system complexity, introducing new failure modes (e.g., failover logic bugs, synchronization failures between redundant units).
RISK: Predictive maintenance requires historical failure data and domain expertise; immature or novel systems may lack sufficient data to train reliable models.
RISK: Condition monitoring adds instrumentation cost and potential points of failure; sensor failures can mask or misdiagnose subsystem health.

RISK: Evaluation period duration and operational profile (duty cycle, environmental stress) significantly impact measured availability; short or benign evaluation periods may not expose latent failures.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly address: (1) Definition of 'operational availability'—does it include planned maintenance windows, or only unplanned downtime? (2) Scope of 'critical subsystems'—which functions must be redundant vs. acceptable to lose? (3) Evaluation period specifics—duration, operational profile, environmental conditions, and acceptance sampling method. (4) Clarify whether 0.97 is system-level or component-level availability. Propose revised FR: 'The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability ≥ 0.97 (measured as uptime excluding planned maintenance) over a [X-month] evaluation period under [specified duty cycle and environmental conditions], with automatic recovery from single-point failures in [list critical subsystems] and mean-time-to-repair ≤ [Y hours] for non-recoverable failures.' This will constrain DP selection and enable quantitative validation of the FR<->DP mapping.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for redundancy management, failover orchestration, condition monitoring, fault detection, and automated diagnostics across all critical subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time sensor data, subsystem health metrics, and operational parameters from Vehicle and Teleoperation for condition monitoring and fault detection.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits failover triggers, fault diagnostics, maintenance recommendations, and safety-critical alerts to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational context and task parameters; returns failover status, subsystem availability, and maintenance scheduling constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates redundancy state, diagnostic results, and predictive maintenance data to NGHE Solution for fleet-level coordination and analytics.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of redundancy management (DP1), condition monitoring (DP3), and fault detection/diagnostics (DP6). All three DPs are safety-critical and preventive in nature. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert/tasking egress required. Cohesion is high—all three DPs work together to achieve fault tolerance and availability.

Poor fit. Vehicle is the subject of redundancy and monitoring, not the owner. Placing DP1, DP3, DP6 here would couple safety logic tightly to vehicle-specific hardware, making it difficult to apply the same strategy across multiple vehicle types or to Teleoperation. High diffusion: redundancy orchestration and diagnostics would leak into vehicle control logic.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a consumer of safety state, not the owner of redundancy and fault management. Placing these DPs here would entangle operator interface logic with low-level subsystem diagnostics and failover mechanics. High diffusion: condition monitoring and failover would scatter across teleoperation UI and command handling.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a higher-level orchestrator and analytics platform, not the real-time safety controller. Placing DP1 (failover) here introduces unacceptable latency for critical subsystem switching. DP3 and DP6 could be partially hosted here for fleet analytics, but reactive failover must be local. High diffusion: real-time safety logic would have to be duplicated or delegated back to Vehicle/Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the single, most specific module that can contain the integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP6) with minimal diffusion. It is semantically aligned with fault tolerance, predictive maintenance, and diagnostics. The module has clear input (Telemetry, Tasking) and output (SafetyAlert, Network Link) interfaces that support the required data flows without scattering logic across multiple owners.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services becomes a monolithic catch-all for all safety logic, it may grow too large and difficult to test. Mitigation: Decompose Safety Services internally into sub-modules (e.g., RedundancyManager, ConditionMonitor, DiagnosticsEngine) with clear internal interfaces.

Risk: Real-time failover (DP1) may require sub-millisecond response times that are incompatible with network latency to NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Ensure Safety Services runs locally on Vehicle or Teleoperation with autonomous failover capability; use Network Link only for post-event reporting and fleet-level learning.

Risk: Condition monitoring (DP3) may require deep knowledge of subsystem-specific health indicators that are not exposed via generic Telemetry interface. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface to include subsystem-specific diagnostic channels, or establish a secondary diagnostic data stream.

Risk: Automated diagnostics (DP6) may require access to detailed logs and state snapshots that are not available through standard interfaces. Mitigation: Define a diagnostic data export capability within Telemetry or create a dedicated diagnostic interface.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP6) is well-suited to Safety Services. However, clarify that Safety Services must operate with local autonomy for failover (DP1) to meet real-time constraints, while condition monitoring (DP3) and diagnostics (DP6) can be augmented by NGHE Solution for fleet-level analytics and predictive insights. This preserves safety-critical responsiveness while enabling proactive fleet management.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 (redundant subsystems + predictive maintenance + fault detection) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR requirement to achieve and maintain availability ≥ 0.97. The integrated approach is sound: DP1 reduces downtime via automatic failover; DP3 reduces failure frequency via condition monitoring; DP6 accelerates recovery via automated diagnostics. The combination is logically coherent and measurable against the acceptance criterion (availability ≥ 0.97 over evaluation period).

diffusion_risks: DP1 (failover) requires sub-millisecond response times. If Safety Services is not co-located with Vehicle or Teleoperation, network latency to NGHE Solution will violate real-time constraints. Mitigation: Enforce local autonomous failover in Safety Services; use Network Link only for post-event reporting.

DP3 (condition monitoring) depends on subsystem-specific health indicators that may not be exposed via the generic Telemetry interface. Risk of incomplete data ingestion leading to missed predictive signals. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface specification to enumerate required diagnostic channels per subsystem.

DP6 (automated diagnostics) requires access to detailed logs, state snapshots, and historical traces that may not be available through standard Telemetry. Risk of diagnostic blind spots. Mitigation: Define diagnostic data export capability or establish a dedicated diagnostic data stream interface.

Safety Services may become a monolithic catch-all if internal decomposition is not enforced. Risk of reduced testability and maintainability. Mitigation: Mandate internal sub-module structure (RedundancyManager, ConditionMonitor, DiagnosticsEngine) with explicit internal interfaces.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary with containment score 0.92 and diffusion score 0.08. All three DPs (DP1, DP3, DP6) are semantically aligned with safety-critical fault tolerance and preventive maintenance—core responsibilities of Safety Services. The module has well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout, Network Link inout) that support required data flows without scattering logic. Vehicle (0.45 containment, 0.55 diffusion), Teleoperation (0.4 containment, 0.6 diffusion), and NGHE Solution (0.35 containment, 0.65 diffusion) are all poor fits due to semantic misalignment and high diffusion risk. No alternative IM boundary is recommended.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability measurement is directly verifiable: collect operational uptime data over the agreed evaluation period and compare against the acceptance criterion (≥ 0.97). Safety Services module provides clear observability points: failover events (DP1), condition monitoring alerts (DP3), and diagnostic reports (DP6) can all be logged and analyzed. Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces provide instrumentation for traceability. Verification is feasible provided that Safety Services exposes sufficient diagnostic telemetry and that the evaluation period is long enough to capture representative failure modes.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation from FR to DP to IM is clear and traceable. FR (availability ≥ 0.97) → DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP6) → IM (Safety Services) forms a coherent chain. Ownership is unambiguous: Safety Services is the single responsible module. However, clarity must be enhanced in three areas: (1) Clarify that Safety Services operates with local autonomy for DP1 (failover) to meet real-time constraints, while DP3 and DP6 can be augmented by NGHE Solution for fleet-level analytics. (2) Specify the Telemetry interface to enumerate subsystem-specific diagnostic channels required by DP3 and DP6. (3) Define internal decomposition of Safety Services (RedundancyManager, ConditionMonitor, DiagnosticsEngine) to prevent monolithic growth and ensure testability.

recommended_revisions: Clarify Safety Services operational model: DP1 (failover) must execute locally with autonomous decision-making to meet sub-millisecond response times. DP3 (condition monitoring) and DP6 (diagnostics) can be augmented by NGHE Solution for fleet-level predictive analytics and learning, but local Safety Services retains primary responsibility for real-time detection and response.

Extend Telemetry interface specification to explicitly enumerate subsystem-specific diagnostic channels (e.g., motor health indicators, hydraulic pressure trends, electrical fault codes) required to support DP3 (condition monitoring) and DP6 (automated diagnostics). Define data refresh rates and precision requirements.

Define diagnostic data export capability within Telemetry interface or establish a dedicated diagnostic data stream to provide Safety Services with access to detailed logs, state snapshots, and historical traces required for DP6 (automated diagnostics).

Mandate internal decomposition of Safety Services into sub-modules: RedundancyManager (owns DP1), ConditionMonitor (owns DP3), DiagnosticsEngine (owns DP6), with explicit internal interfaces and clear separation of concerns. This prevents monolithic growth and improves testability.

Establish acceptance criteria for each DP component: DP1 failover latency ≤ X ms; DP3 condition monitoring detection accuracy ≥ Y%; DP6 diagnostic root-cause identification success rate ≥ Z%. These sub-criteria support verification of the overall availability target (≥ 0.97).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the gate results expose critical architectural and operational dependencies that cannot be resolved at the current level of abstraction. Specifically: (1) DP1 (failover) requires sub-millisecond autonomous response, which demands explicit decomposition of Safety Services into a RedundancyManager sub-module with its own FR-DP-IM chain; (2) DP3 (condition monitoring) and DP6 (diagnostics) depend on Telemetry interface extensions that are not yet specified, creating a diffusion risk that must be resolved through sub-level FR derivation; (3) The internal structure of Safety Services is mandated but not yet designed, leaving testability and maintainability risks unmitigated. Given high safety criticality, proceeding without explicit sub-level decomposition violates the principle of design clarity and traceability. Zigzag down to decompose Safety Services into its constituent functional responsibilities and to specify the Telemetry interface requirements that support DP3 and DP6.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Detect subsystem failures and execute autonomous failover within sub-millisecond latency without dependency on external network or NGHE Solution.
FR1.2: Monitor subsystem health indicators in real-time and generate condition-based alerts when degradation is detected, enabling predictive maintenance decisions.
FR1.3: Collect, correlate, and analyze diagnostic data (logs, state snapshots, historical traces) to identify root causes of failures and support automated recovery recommendations.

FR1.4: Expose subsystem-specific diagnostic channels through the Telemetry interface, including motor health indicators, hydraulic pressure trends, electrical fault codes, and sensor anomalies, with defined refresh rates and precision requirements.

FR1.5: Maintain internal architectural separation between failover logic (RedundancyManager), condition monitoring logic (ConditionMonitor), and diagnostics logic (DiagnosticsEngine) to ensure testability, maintainability, and clear ownership boundaries within Safety Services.

revise.why: The current DP specification (DP1 + DP3 + DP6) is logically sound but operationally underspecified. Before decomposing Safety Services, clarify the operational model: (1) DP1 must be autonomous and local; DP3 and DP6 can be augmented by NGHE Solution for fleet-level analytics but Safety Services retains primary responsibility. (2) Specify that DP3 and DP6 depend on extended Telemetry interface with enumerated diagnostic channels. (3) Explicitly state that Safety Services internal decomposition (RedundancyManager, ConditionMonitor, DiagnosticsEngine) is a design constraint, not a recommendation. These clarifications will guide sub-level FR derivation and prevent diffusion during implementation.

assumptions: Safety Services can be decomposed into three internally coherent sub-modules (RedundancyManager, ConditionMonitor, DiagnosticsEngine) without violating real-time constraints or introducing new failure modes.
Telemetry interface can be extended to enumerate subsystem-specific diagnostic channels without creating unacceptable data volume or latency overhead.
Sub-millisecond failover latency is achievable through local autonomous decision-making in Safety Services without requiring hardware-level redundancy beyond what is already specified in DP1.
Condition monitoring (DP3) and diagnostics (DP6) can operate with incomplete or degraded diagnostic data streams without compromising the overall availability target (≥ 0.97).
NGHE Solution can be augmented to consume Safety Services diagnostic outputs for fleet-level learning without creating feedback loops or race conditions that compromise local safety decisions.
open_risks: DP1 failover latency: If Safety Services is not co-located with Vehicle or Teleoperation, network latency to NGHE Solution may violate sub-millisecond constraint. Mitigation requires explicit architectural decision on Safety Services placement and autonomous failover scope.

Telemetry interface completeness: DP3 and DP6 depend on diagnostic channels that may not be exposed by all subsystems. Risk of incomplete data ingestion leading to missed predictive signals or diagnostic blind spots. Mitigation requires enumeration of required diagnostic channels and subsystem-level interface compliance.

Safety Services monolithic growth: Without explicit internal decomposition and interface contracts, Safety Services may accumulate unrelated responsibilities (e.g., fleet analytics, user notifications) that degrade testability and maintainability. Mitigation requires mandate for internal sub-module structure and clear separation of concerns.

Verification feasibility: Availability measurement requires representative failure modes to occur during evaluation period. If evaluation period is too short or failure modes are rare, verification may be inconclusive. Mitigation requires definition of synthetic failure injection or extended evaluation period.

Diffusion of diagnostic logic: DP6 (automated diagnostics) may require access to detailed logs and state snapshots that are scattered across multiple subsystems. Risk of diagnostic logic diffusion across module boundaries. Mitigation requires definition of diagnostic data export capability or dedicated diagnostic data stream interface.
evidence_needed: Specification of Safety Services internal decomposition: RedundancyManager (owns DP1), ConditionMonitor (owns DP3), DiagnosticsEngine (owns DP6), with explicit internal interfaces and clear separation of concerns.

Extended Telemetry interface specification: Enumerate subsystem-specific diagnostic channels (motor health, hydraulic pressure, electrical faults, sensor anomalies) with defined refresh rates, precision requirements, and data volume estimates.

Failover latency analysis: Demonstrate that autonomous failover in Safety Services can achieve sub-millisecond response time without external network dependency. Provide latency budget breakdown (detection, decision, actuation).

Condition monitoring algorithm specification: Define health indicator thresholds, anomaly detection methods, and alert generation logic for DP3. Specify false positive/negative rates and detection latency.

Diagnostic data export specification: Define diagnostic data stream interface (format, protocol, refresh rate) that provides Safety Services with access to logs, state snapshots, and historical traces required for DP6.

Acceptance criteria for sub-DPs: DP1 failover latency ≤ X ms; DP3 condition monitoring detection accuracy ≥ Y%; DP6 diagnostic root-cause identification success rate ≥ Z%. These sub-criteria support verification of overall availability target (≥ 0.97).

Evaluation plan: Define evaluation period, failure injection strategy, and measurement methodology for verifying availability ≥ 0.97. Specify how synthetic failures will be used to accelerate verification if natural failure modes are rare.
